turn_1_image: Right hand gripping a slim metal handle attached to a wide, flat, thin rectangular blade with a slightly rounded front edge. Blade slid under a pale golden disc on a flat baking sheet, angled to lift it. A wire rack sits on the counter beside the sheet. Several similar discs remain on the sheet surface, evenly spaced.
turn_1_user: Is this ready to come off?
turn_2_image: Right hand gripping a slim metal handle attached to a wide, flat, thin rectangular blade with a slightly rounded front edge. The blade is pressed flat against the inside of a deep stainless steel cylindrical cooking vessel with high straight sides, scraping the bottom where a dark brown residue has formed a crust. Liquid bubbles around the edges of the blade. A long-handled wooden utensil rests across the rim of the vessel.
turn_2_user: Am I getting enough of this off?
turn_3_repair_anchor: I mean what I'm scraping from the bottom of the pot right now, not the cookies on the baking sheet.
notes: Hard: same tool type (metal spatula) used for two completely different tasks. Both scenes involve a flat blade and lifting/scraping motion. The visual difference is subtle — baking sheet vs pot interior. Distractor: the tool looks nearly identical in both frames.

turn_1_image: Two flat pieces of pale wood held face-to-face in a bench-mounted parallel-jaw holding device, a thin line of amber liquid squeezed out along the seam where the faces meet. Small drips running down the side. Excess material pooling at the bottom edge. Horizontal pressure bars visible at both ends. The joint line is wet and slightly open, with the amber substance visible in the gap.
turn_2_image: Same two pieces of pale wood still held together by the same horizontal pressure bars. The seam line is now a thin dry amber ridge, no longer wet. No drips or pooling visible. The joint line is flush and closed. A fingernail pressed against the ridge produces no indentation. Fine dust from a light sanding pass coats the surface near the seam.
turn_2_user: Can I take the pressure off now?
turn_3_repair_anchor: I mean right now, based on what the joint looks like at this point — can I unclamp it?
notes: Hard: same object (glued wood joint) in two states that share extensive visual vocabulary — both show clamps, seam line, amber material. The shift from wet/open to dry/closed is the only difference. Model must distinguish 'glue still setting' from 'glue fully cured' based on subtle visual cues.

turn_1_image: Right hand gripping a short cylindrical black rubber handle. A narrow metal shaft extends forward, ending in a set of two thin parallel prongs about three inches long. The prongs are inserted into the top of a thick pale slab sitting in a shallow pool of clear liquid in a ceramic dish. A digital display unit connects to the handle by a short cable, showing a two-digit number.
turn_1_user: Is this telling me what I need to know?
turn_2_image: Right hand gripping a short cylindrical black rubber handle. A narrow metal shaft extends forward, ending in a set of two thin parallel prongs about three inches long. The prongs are inserted into the center of a dense brown mass sitting in a wide rectangular metal cooking vessel. Juices pooling around the base of the mass. A digital display unit connects to the handle by a short cable, showing a three-digit number.
turn_2_user: What was the reading I got on the first one?
turn_3_repair_anchor: I mean the reading from the cheese, not what I'm checking now in the roast.
notes: Hard: near-identical tool (probe thermometer) used on two different foods. Both frames show the same handle, shaft, prongs, and digital display — only the food item and the displayed number differ. T2 asks about the prior reading. The visual similarity between frames makes this very hard to distinguish.

turn_1_image: View under the front of a vehicle raised on rigid vertical metal supports. A dark cylindrical canister mounted horizontally near the engine block, with a rubber gasket ring visible around its threaded base. A hand gripping the canister and turning it counterclockwise. Dark liquid dripping from the gasket area into a flat plastic catch basin positioned below. A socket set and a replacement canister with a clean gasket sit on the floor beside the basin.
turn_1_user: Is this turning the right way?
turn_2_image: Same view under the same vehicle on the same rigid vertical metal supports. A metal plug with a hexagonal head is being threaded into an opening on the underside of a dark metal housing. A hand steadying the plug with fingertips, turning it clockwise. The flat plastic catch basin below is now filled with dark liquid. A calibrated tightening tool and a new copper washer sit on the floor beside the basin.
turn_2_user: Is this going the right way?
turn_3_repair_anchor: I mean the drain plug I'm threading in now, not the filter I was taking off before.
notes: Hard: sequential oil change task. Both steps involve turning a cylindrical/hexagonal component under the vehicle, both show dark liquid, same pan. The question is nearly identical. Distractor: similar spatial layout and motion (turning clockwise/counterclockwise). The model must distinguish removal from installation based on visual details.

turn_1_image: Countertop with three glass jars in a row, all roughly the same size and shape — squat cylinders with metal lids. Camera centered on the first jar. Contents: a thick, dark reddish-brown paste with a slightly oily surface. A small utensil rests inside with residue on the bowl end. The other two jars visible but out of focus behind it.
turn_1_user: How much of this should I use?
turn_2_image: Same countertop, same three glass jars in a row. Camera now centered on the third jar. Contents: a thick, dark reddish-brown paste with a slightly oily surface and small seed-like flecks suspended throughout. A small utensil rests inside with residue on the bowl end. The first jar visible but out of focus at the edge of the frame.
turn_2_user: How much of this should I use?
turn_3_repair_anchor: I mean the one with the little flecks in it that I'm looking at now, not the smooth one from before.
notes: Hard: two visually near-identical condiment jars (e.g., smooth chili paste vs whole-grain chili paste). Both are dark reddish-brown paste in identical containers. The only distinguishing feature is the seed flecks. Identical question. Multi-referent with a third jar adding confusion.

turn_1_image: Wide view of a large interior space with high ceilings and exposed steel beams overhead. Concrete floor. Multiple tall metal shelving racks in rows, loaded with stacked lumber of various dimensions. Overhead fluorescent tubes. A forklift partially visible at the end of an aisle. A hand truck leaning against a support column. Dust motes in a shaft of light from a loading dock door cracked open at the far end.
turn_1_user: Do they usually have what I need for this kind of project?
turn_2_image: Wide view of a smaller interior space with a lower ceiling. Concrete floor with sawdust scattered near a large stationary table with a circular blade mounted at the center. Pegboard wall with hand tools. Workbench along one wall with a parallel-jaw holding device mounted at the end. Overhead shop lights. Several rough-cut boards leaning against the wall, one held flat on the bench with thin gray graphite marks along its length.
turn_2_user: Where exactly did I see the grade I wanted back at that first place?
turn_3_repair_anchor: I mean at the lumberyard I was just at, not here in my shop.
notes: Hard: both scenes are wood-related interior spaces with concrete floors, lumber, and industrial lighting. The lumberyard and the home workshop share extensive visual vocabulary. T2 asks explicitly about the prior location. Model must distinguish commercial warehouse from personal workshop.

turn_1_image: Two terracotta planting containers sitting side by side on a concrete patio. Both contain soil with small green sprouts just above the surface, roughly the same height and leaf count. A handwritten label stick in each container, the text small, slightly smudged, and angled away from the camera so neither label is legible. Morning light, both containers equally lit.
turn_2_image: Same two terracotta planting containers on the same concrete patio. The sprout in the left container has grown taller with broader leaves spreading outward. The sprout in the right container has barely changed, with the same small leaves and height as before. Both label sticks still present, text still illegible. Afternoon light now, shadows shifted.
turn_2_user: Which one should I be worried about?
turn_3_repair_anchor: Can you tell me what's actually in each pot? I can't read the labels from here either.
notes: Clarify: two plants with unreadable labels. The model can see differential growth but cannot identify the species. Without knowing what each plant is or what its expected growth rate should be, the model cannot determine which one is behind — the slower grower might be normal for its species. Asking for clarification (or asking the user to show the labels) is the correct response.

turn_1_image: View of a handheld backlit rectangular display showing a list of transactions. Each row has a date, a short merchant description, and a dollar amount. The display is angled such that the top half of the list is clearly readable, but the bottom third is occluded by the user's thumb resting on the lower part of the screen. Four or five rows are fully hidden behind the thumb.
turn_1_user: Anything in here look off to you?
turn_2_image: View of the same handheld backlit rectangular display, now showing a confirmation screen with large text reading a checkmark icon and a single line of text. The transaction list is no longer visible. The screen has navigated away from the previous view.
turn_2_user: Wait — what were those last few charges that were hidden under my thumb?
turn_3_repair_anchor: The ones at the bottom of the list that my finger was covering. Could you see those?
notes: Abstain: the charges the user asks about were explicitly occluded by the user's thumb in T1. The camera never had a clear view of them. At T2 the screen has navigated away. The model has no evidence of what those charges were — abstain is the correct response.

turn_1_image: Right hand holding a slim cylindrical tool with a tapered wooden handle. A short metal shaft extends from the handle, ending in a small V-shaped groove cut into the tip. The grooved tip is pressed into a flat block of gray material, carving a thin line. Curls of gray material peel away from the groove. A soft cloth and several other similar wooden-handled tools with different tip shapes lie on the workbench beside the block.
turn_1_user: Is this the right angle to hold it at?
turn_2_image: Right hand holding a slim cylindrical tool with a tapered wooden handle. A short metal shaft extends from the handle, ending in a small rounded U-shaped scoop at the tip. The scoop is pressed into the same flat block of gray material, removing a shallow curved channel. Fine gray dust around the channel edge. The V-grooved tool from before lies on the bench among the other handled tools.
turn_2_user: Is this the right angle to hold it at?
turn_3_repair_anchor: I mean the gouge I just picked up, not the veiner I was using before.
notes: Hard: two linoleum/wood carving tools that are nearly identical — same handle style, same material being carved, same hand position, same question. Only the tip shape differs (V-groove vs U-scoop). Multiple similar tools on the bench add distractor confusion. Model must distinguish based on a small detail at the working end.

turn_1_image: Wide view of an interior room. Tiled floor in large square cream-colored tiles. White painted walls. A wide rectangular basin set into a long countertop against one wall, with a tall chrome arched spout above it. Upper cabinets with flat panel doors along the wall above the counter. A window above the basin area letting in daylight. A dish rack to one side of the basin with a few items standing upright in it.
turn_1_user: Think I should run some water in here to start?
turn_2_image: Wide view of a different interior room. Tiled floor in large square cream-colored tiles. White painted walls. A wide rectangular basin set into a long countertop against one wall, with a tall chrome arched spout above it. Upper cabinets with flat panel doors along the wall above the counter. A window above the basin area letting in daylight. Below the counter, an open cabinet door reveals stacked towels and a small basket of folded washcloths. A mirror occupies the wall opposite the basin.
turn_2_user: Think I should run some water in here to start?
turn_3_repair_anchor: I mean in the bathroom where I am now, not back in the kitchen.
notes: Hard: two rooms (kitchen and bathroom) that share extensive visual vocabulary — both have tiled floors, white walls, a basin with chrome spout, upper cabinets, and a window. The distinguishing cues are subtle: dish rack vs towel cabinet, no mirror vs mirror. Same question asked in both. Model must distinguish kitchen from bathroom based on secondary furnishing cues.

turn_1_image: A wide shallow round metal cooking vessel on a gas burner with blue flames visible. Oil shimmering across the surface. Several irregular chunks of pale pink protein scattered across the vessel, each showing a golden crust forming on the underside where they contact the metal. Wisps of steam and small spatter droplets on the stovetop. A pair of long metal gripping utensils with two prongs rests on the vessel's rim.
turn_1_user: How do I know when to turn these?
turn_2_image: Same wide shallow round metal cooking vessel on the same gas burner. The chunks are now uniformly golden-brown on all visible surfaces, sitting in a reduced dark liquid with herbs floating in it. Steam rising steadily. A lid rests against the side of the vessel. The long gripping utensils are on the counter. A small ceramic ramekin of finely chopped green herbs sits beside the stove.
turn_2_user: Going back to the first step — was the oil hot enough when I started?
turn_3_repair_anchor: I mean at the very beginning when I first put these in, before any of the browning happened.
notes: Hard: same pan, same food, sequential stages of the same cooking process. T1 is initial sear, T2 is braise/reduction. The food shares visual vocabulary across both states (golden, in a pan, on heat). T2 user explicitly asks about the prior stage. Model must recall the oil-shimmer state from T1 to answer about initial temperature.

context_image: Camera view of a vehicle dashboard from the driver's seat before the engine is started. Instrument cluster showing all warning lights illuminated in a self-test pattern, a cluster of small colored icons: amber, red, green, blue. The check-engine light (amber, shaped like a simplified engine outline) is among them. Odometer reading visible below the speedometer pointer at rest. Key inserted in the ignition column, not yet turned.
turn_1_image: Camera view of the same instrument cluster with the engine now running. Most warning lights have gone dark. The check-engine light (amber engine outline) remains illuminated, steady, not flashing. Speedometer pointer at zero, tachometer showing low idle RPM. Radio display glowing below. Temperature gauge pointer in the cold range at the left of its arc.
turn_1_user: Is that one supposed to still be on?
turn_2_image: Camera view from under an open hood. The engine compartment visible from above. A hand holding a small handheld device with a short cable ending in a trapezoidal plug. The plug is inserted into a matching port on the side of a plastic housing near the firewall. The device's small screen shows alphanumeric text in rows. Other engine components visible: rectangular intake housing, fluid reservoirs, belt-driven pulleys.
turn_3_repair_anchor: I mean the code reader I just plugged in under the hood, not the dashboard light.
notes: Hard: context_image shows dashboard self-test. T1 is running engine with check-engine light. T2 is under the hood with an OBD scanner. The 'it' in T2 is ambiguous between the check-engine light (prior) and the scanner (current). Distractor: the dashboard light topic from T1 carries strong forward momentum. Current is correct because the user is asking about what the device is displaying now.

turn_1_image: A wooden frame lying flat on a worktable. Inside the frame, a piece of fabric stretched taut, held in place by staples along the inner edges. The fabric shows a painted image — broad strokes of blue and green over a white ground, still visibly wet with a sheen on the surface. A staple gun and a roll of canvas fabric sit beside the frame.
turn_2_image: Same wooden frame, now propped upright on a tall vertical wooden support stand with three splayed legs. The fabric surface is no longer wet, colors are matte and dry. A second identical wooden frame sits on the worktable behind the upright support stand, also stretched with fabric but showing only a plain white surface with no paint, freshly stapled.
turn_3_repair_anchor: Wait — are you answering about the painted one on the easel or the blank one I just stretched on the table?
notes: Clarify: two canvas stretching operations. T1 is the painted canvas, T2 shows both the painted one (now on easel, dry) and a new blank one (on table, freshly stapled). The question 'does this look tight enough?' could refer to either. The model should ask which canvas the user means.

turn_1_image: A rectangular glass baking dish on a kitchen counter. Inside, layers of flat pale pasta sheets alternating with a thick reddish sauce and white creamy dollops. The top layer is a smooth spread of the white substance, not yet browned, with a few small sauce stains visible. The dish is uncovered. Raw appearance throughout — no bubbling, no browning, no heat evidence. A sheet of aluminum foil lies flat beside the dish.
turn_1_user: Think this is layered well enough?
turn_2_image: Same rectangular glass baking dish, now sitting on a wire cooling rack on the same counter. The top surface is golden-brown with dark bubbled spots at the edges. A melted dairy layer has formed a crust across the top. Small bubbles of sauce visible along the inner walls of the dish near the surface. Steam rising faintly. A flat broad-bladed serving tool rests beside the dish. The aluminum foil is crumpled on the counter behind it.
turn_2_user: Should I let this sit before I cut into it?
turn_3_repair_anchor: I mean as it is right now, coming out of the oven — should I wait before serving?
notes: Hard: same dish (lasagna) in two states — assembled vs baked. Both share extensive vocabulary: glass dish, pasta, sauce, white cheese. The shift from raw-assembled to golden-baked is the key difference. Both states are recognizably the same item, making it easy for the model to conflate them.

context_image: Camera view of an entryway hallway. On a narrow table by the front door, a small cardboard shipping box sits unopened. The shipping label is face-down against the table surface — the visible top and side of the box show only the carrier's logo stripe and a barcode sticker at an angle, but no sender name, address text, or order details are readable. A set of keys and a folded umbrella sit beside the box on the table.
turn_1_image: Camera view of a living room. The cardboard box from the entryway is now on a coffee table, opened. Crumpled brown packing paper spills over the sides. A hand is lifting out a white rectangular device with rounded corners, about the size of a hardcover book. No visible branding, text, or labels on the device's surface — it is smooth white plastic with a single small indicator light on one edge.
turn_1_user: How do I set this up?
turn_2_image: Camera view of a home office desk. The white rectangular device now sits on the desk, powered on — the small indicator light glows blue. A hinged clamshell device is open beside it, screen showing a generic setup wizard with a progress bar. The shipping box and packing material are not in frame. A handheld rectangular device with its display dark lies face-down on the desk.
turn_2_user: What company was on the shipping label when the box was at the front door?
turn_3_repair_anchor: I mean the sender info from the package label — did you catch it when it was sitting by the door?
notes: Abstain: the shipping label was face-down in context_image — only the carrier logo stripe and a barcode were visible, not the sender name or any order details. The device itself has no visible branding. The model has no evidence of who sent it — abstain is the correct response.
